narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your background includes a middle-income family, full-time student status, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, an LSAT score of 32, and school decile rankings of 3rd in both your first and third years—below the typical student's averages of around 5th to 6th decile, 3.21 GPA, and 36.7 LSAT. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated your chance of failing the bar at 46.1%, tipping the scales toward rejection to minimize the risk of future struggles. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your third-year school decile ranking, as being in the lower third stood out strongly compared to most students who rank higher and tend to pass. This was the most influential element in the model's cautious decision. On a more positive note, your middle family income actually nudged the prediction toward success, performing better than lower brackets that often correlate with higher failure rates. Similarly, your LSAT score and first-year decile, though below average, contributed somewhat toward passing by not being in the absolute lowest ranges. Your undergraduate GPA had a minor push toward failure risk, while planning full-time study—which most students do—helped slightly in the model's eyes. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by lagging in key academic deciles and test scores, which outweighed the supportive aspects like your income and study commitment. While this rejection stings, especially given the barriers Black women often navigate in legal education, it doesn't define your potential. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits, retaking the LSAT for a higher score, and perhaps gaining relevant experience or recommendations that highlight your resilience. Keep pushing forward—you have the drive to rewrite this story.
